coredata.dc:title: Polyvinylpyrrolidone in the one-step synthesis of carbon quantum dots anchored hollow microsphere Bi2WO6 enhances the simultaneous photocatalytic removal of tetracycline and Cr (VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chaoqun
Zhao, Ziqing
Fu, Shaozhu
Wang, Xingyue
Ma, Yuhan
Dong, Shuangshi
coredata.dc:description:
The development of visible-light-responsive catalysts that are capable of simultaneous photo-oxidation and photo-reduction has been a major focus of research. In this study, carbon quantum dots anchored hollow microsphere Bi2WO6 (CQDs/HBWO) photocatalyst was successfully prepared via a one-step polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP)-assisted hydrothermal method. PVP had a dual function: controlling hollow microsphere morphologies as well as serving as a precursor of carbon quantum dots. The as-prepared CQDs/HBWO-18 had the highest catalytic activity for the photocatalytic degradation of TCH (0.02768 h −1), which was approximately 2.1 and 1.23 times higher than that for the hollow microsphere Bi2WO6 without CQDs and the nanosheet Bi2WO6, respectively. Only CQDs/HBWO-18 was effective for the photoreduction of Cr (VI) in the system without pH adjustment or scavenger addition. The photoreduction efficiency of Cr (VI) increased 83% and 58% in the bi-pollutant system with 5 mg/L and 10 mg/L of Cr (VI) containing 20 mg/L TCH compared with Cr (VI) alone. The improved photoreduction activity of Cr (VI) could be attributed to TCH acting as a photogenerated hole scavenger. The results of the scavenging test and electron spin resonance confirmed that h+,
O2 − and
OH were the main reactive species. Eventually, the possible degradation pathway of TCH was proposed.

O2 − and DMPO−

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The development of visible-light-responsive catalysts that are capable of simultaneous photo-oxidation and photo-reduction has been a major focus of research. In this study, carbon quantum dots anchored hollow microsphere Bi2WO6 (CQDs/HBWO) photocatalyst was successfully prepared via a one-step polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP)-assisted hydrothermal method. PVP had a dual function: controlling hollow microsphere morphologies as well as serving as a precursor of carbon quantum dots. The as-prepared CQDs/HBWO-18 had the highest catalytic activity for the photocatalytic degradation of TCH (0.02768 h −1), which was approximately 2.1 and 1.23 times higher than that for the hollow microsphere Bi2WO6 without CQDs and the nanosheet Bi2WO6, respectively. Only CQDs/HBWO-18 was effective for the photoreduction of Cr (VI) in the system without pH adjustment or scavenger addition. The photoreduction efficiency of Cr (VI) increased 83% and 58% in the bi-pollutant system with 5 mg/L and 10 mg/L of Cr (VI) containing 20 mg/L TCH compared with Cr (VI) alone. The improved photoreduction activity of Cr (VI) could be attributed to TCH acting as a photogenerated hole scavenger. The results of the scavenging test and electron spin resonance confirmed that h+,
O2 − and
OH were the main reactive species. Eventually, the possible degradation pathway of TCH was proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution by organic contaminants, such as antibiotics (e.g., tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH)), heavy metals (e.g., Chromium (Cr (VI))) and microorganisms (e.g., Escherichia coli), has become a severe environmental problem that poses a serious threat to human health and aquatic ecosystems [1–3]. TCH belongs to a group of broad-spectrum antibiotics and is widely used for its high germicidal performance and cost-effectiveness. A large amount of residual TCH is discharged into the water environment, which strains the water environment because of its high stability [4–6]. Cr (VI) is a widespread heavy metal pollutant. Cr (VI)-containing water is carcinogenic, teratogenic and mutagenic to animals and human beings [7,8]. Furthermore, Cr (VI) is often discharged along with hazardous organic contaminants, which makes it even more challenging to remove. There is thus an urgent need to develop efficient and environmentally friendly strategies for the simultaneous removal of the aforementioned pollutants in wastewater. Photocatalysis is considered a promising approach for resolving this environmental issue [9–11]. The one-pot removal of pollutants from wastewater under light irradiation has been achieved by some metal oxide and metallic disulfide photocatalysts, such as TiO2/BiOI [12], Fe3O4/ZnO [13], ZrO2 [14], Pt-N/F-TiO2 [15], AgI/BiVO4 [16], RP/SnS2 [17], RP/TiO2 [18], and RP-MoS2/rGO [19]. However, the wide band gaps, not environmentally friendly and high cost of photocatalysts limit their application. The development of efficient, visible-light-driven and economically feasible photocatalysts for the simultaneous removal of multi-pollutants in wastewater would be an ideal strategy for ensuring a sustainable future. Bismuth-based materials have gotten lots of attention owing to the suitable band gap and excellent photocatalytic performance. Among various Bi-based photocatalyst, bismuth tungstate (Bi2WO6), one of the simplest Aurivillius oxides, has received much interest for its high photocatalytic performance under visible-light irradiation. Bi2WO6 is composed of the alternate stacking of [Bi2O2]2+ and octahedral [WO4]2− layers with oxygen atoms sharing adjacent layers. The special layered structure benefits the separation of electrons and holes and leads to enhanced photocatalytic activity [20–22]. The activity can be further improved by co-catalysts and morphology control [23,24].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a new class of carbon nanomaterials, which have made promising application in photocatalysis owing to their unique chemical and optical properties. Very recently, CQDs have been used for modifying the Bi-based photocatalysts to promote photocatalytic degradation efficiency of TCH, such as CQDs/Bi2MoO6 [25], CQDs/BiOI [26] and CQDs/BiOBr [27]. These studies have shown that the photocatalytic degradation performance of CQD Bi-based photocatalysts was higher than that of pure Bi-based photocatalysts, which was attributed to the interfacial transfer of photogenerated electrons from semiconductors to CQDs. Thus, the anchoring of CQDs onto Bi-based photocatalysts is considered a promising way of improving the photocatalytic activity. However, the photoreduction of Cr (VI) by CQD/Bi2WO6 has been rarely studied, especially for the simultaneous removal of coexisting pollutants in wastewater. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) has been used as a precursor to obtain CQDs [28]. Nevertheless, most of the studies only considered PVP as a surfactant to control the morphology and crystal structure of Bi2WO6 with nanoplates, polyhedron-like assemblies or hollow microspheres [29]. The morphology greatly affects the photocatalytic performance of Bi2WO6. Bi2WO6 hollow microstructures have shown superior photocatalytic performance because these structures possess a high specific surface area, show high permeability and feature enhanced light utilization because of the multiple reflections of light in the interior [30,31]. We thus hypothesize that PVP can not only control the crystal growth of Bi2WO6 but can also serve as a precursor of CQDs in a one-step synthesis process.

In this study, we developed a surfactant (PVP)-assisted strategy for fabricating 3D CQDs/Bi2WO6 hollow microspheres via a one-step hydrothermal method. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Raman spectra were conducted to investigate in detail to infer the growth process of the composites. The photocatalytic activities of the heterostructures were tested by the separate and simultaneous degradation of TCH and the reduction of Cr (VI) under visible light, respectively. Pure 3D Bi2WO6 hollow microspheres (HBWO) were obtained and compared to characterize the role of COQs in CHBWO. This study provides new insights into the effects of CQDs-decorated HBWO as well as the design of CQDs-based heterostructures with high-performance, visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity for water treatment. The photocatalytic mechanism of the catalyst was proposed.

All the reagents used in this work were purchased without any further purification. CQDs/Bi2WO6 hollow microsphere (CQDs/HBWO) were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method. Specifically, Bi (NO3)3·5H2O (2 mmol) was initially dissolved in ethylene glycol (EG, 15 mL) under vigorous stirring at room temperature to obtain solution A. Na2WO4·2H2O (1 mmol) and polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP K-30, 100 mg) were dissolved in the mixture of ethylene glycol (5 mL) and ethanol (10 mL) under vigorous stirring at room temperature to obtain solution B. Then, the solution A was poured into the solution B under stirring and transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was maintained at 160 °C for different time (6, 12 and 18 h). The resulting precipitate was collected by centrifugation, followed by successive rinsing with absolute ethanol and deionized water for several times. The collected products were dried at 80 °C for 12 h, and were denoted as CQDs/HBWO-6, CQDs/HBWO-12 and CQDs/HBWO-18, respectively. In contrast, the nanosheet Bi2WO6 (NBWO) was prepared by the same procedure except adding PVP. Meanwhile, the pure hollow microsphere Bi2WO6 (HBWO) was obtained by removing the CQDs with the calcination of CQDs/HBWO-18 at 450 °C for 3 h.

The crystal structures and phase data of the as-synthesized samples were obtained on X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8, Germany) using Cu Kα radiation. The Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-7001F), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM) (via a 200 kV FEI-Tecnai F20, USA) were used to investigate the morphology and crystal structure of obtained materials. The chemical states was performed via an X-ray photo-electron spectrometer (XPS) with Al Kαr addition (h = 1486.6 eV). The binding energy data was calibrated against the C 1s signal at 284.6 eV. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded on a spectrophotometer (Lambda 750, Perking Elmer). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed by using a Spectrum One Perkin Elmer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained in a luminescence spectrometer (Cary Eclipse, Varian Medical Systems, USA). The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were performed on a ESR spectrometer (JES-FA200, JOEL) using the spin-trap reagent DMPO (5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide, Sigma Chemical Co.) in water (for hydroxyl radical) and methanol (for superoxide radical).

A Xenon lamp (CEL-HXF 300, Beijing Education Au-light Co., Ltd.) with a 420 nm cutoff filter was applied as the light source. The photocatalytic activates of as-prepared catalysts were evaluated by the degradation of colorless organic contaminant TCH and reduction Cr (VI) under visible-light (420 nm < λ < 780 nm) irradiation. Typically, 10 mg of photocatalyst was added into a quartz reactor which contained 50 mL TCH (20 mg/L) or 50 mg of photocatalyst was added into a quartz reactor which contained 50 mL Cr (VI) (10 mg/L) aqueous solution. The pH of Cr (VI) solution was adjusted to a range of 3–9 with 2 M HCl or NaOH. In addition, 0.5 mL at 2 g/L of tartaric acid (TA) solution (hole scavenger) was added to the reaction, when the pollutant only contained Cr (VI). The adsorption/desorption equilibrium between the target pollutant solution and the catalyst was achieved after magnetic stirring in dark for 60 min. During the photocatalytic tests, 3 mL of suspension was taken out at a certain time interval, followed by immediate centrifugation to remove the photocatalyst particles. The concentration of Cr (VI) during the reaction was calorimetrically monitored at 540 nm using the standard diphenylcarbazide method. The TCH concentration was analyzed by an Agilent 1100 HPLC with 4.6 × 250 mm, 5 μm Venusil HILIC column and UV detector (λ = 356 nm). 0.02 M acetic acid/acetonitrile (90:10, v/v) was used as the mobile phase at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1. The simultaneous photocatalytic degradation of TCH and of reduction Cr (VI) was performed in a reactor containing 20 mg/L TCH and different concentration (5 or 10 mg/L) of Cr (VI) without addition of hole scavenger. The initial pH was not adjusted (around 5.4) and the total volume was 50 mL containing 10 mg of photocatalyst. In addition, the degradation intermediate products were detected by LC-MS system (the characterization methods presented in Text S1).
The disinfection performance of prepared catalysts was evaluated by the inactivation of coli (E. coli) K-12 (TG1 strain). The detailed procedure was listed in Text S1.

For the free radical scavenging activity test, various scavengers were added into the solution of TCH. Specifically, 1 mM disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na), 1 mM p-benzoquinone (BQ), 0.5 mM isopropanol and 0.5 mM KBrO3 were selected as the scavengers for holes (h+), superoxide radicals (
O2 −), hydroxyl radicals (
OH) and electrons (e−), respectively. The scavenging reaction conditions were similar to the photodegradation reactions except the addition of radical scavengers into the reaction solution.

The reusability and stability of the photocatalysts was evaluated by measuring their photocatalytic performance after five cycles. The catalyst was washed with water and ethanol after each photocatalytic reaction cycles, and then re-dispersed into fresh 50 mL TCH (20 mgL−1) solution under the same experimental conditions as mentioned above.

The transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the different samples were carried out on an electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode system (CHI660E, Chenhua Instruments Co. Shanghai, China). Pt wire and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The working electrode was the FTO glass coated by as-prepared 5 mg of catalyst film. The data was measured in 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution under illumination from a 150 W Xenon lamp with a cutoff filter (780 nm > λ > 420 nm).

EtOH and EG were used as the reactive solvent in the preparation of CQDs/HBWO. The Bi-EtOH-EG complexes formed by Bi3+ with reactive solvent before hydrothermal treatment were characterized by FT-IR spectra. The OH stretching vibrational peak (3343 cm−1) of EG and EtOH decreased after the addition of Bi3+ ions with magnetic stirring for 30 min (Fig. 1 a), which suggested that the Bi3+ ions, EG and EtOH together formed Bi-EG-EtOH complexes. The FT-IR peak appearing at 1593 cm−1 for samples with 6 h of solvothermal treatment was ascribed to the symmetrical and anti-symmetrical absorption peaks of the CO and π bonds in the carboxyl group [32] (Fig. 1a). After hydrothermal treatment for 6 h, the stretching vibrational peaks at 889 cm−1 and 1051 cm−1 associated with the OH groups of EG and EtOH weakened in the Bi/EO/EtO samples. EG and EtOH were transformed to their oxidation products (EO and EtO, respectively), which means that the Bi-EG-EtOH complexes tended to transform into Bi/EO/EtO complexes rather than into BWO. This is verified by the fact that there were no characteristic reflections of BWO in the XRD profile (Fig. 1b). When the solvothermal reaction time was increased to 18 h, the carbonyl absorption at 1665 cm−1 of PVP disappeared, and the appearance of new absorption peaks between 2800 cm−1 and 3000 cm−1 was attributed to the CH stretching vibration (Fig. 1a). PVP thus appeared to be transformed into a new compound in the hydrothermal process, and this new compound can be removed via calcination at 450 °C for 3 h. The diffraction peaks in the XRD patterns (Fig. 1b) were indexed to the orthorhombic Bi2WO6 phase (JCPDS, No. 79-2381) [33]. Strong XRD diffraction peaks indicated that both CQDs/HBWO-18 and HBWO were highly crystallized and were characterized by a homogeneous phase with typical crystal planes of orthorhombic Bi2WO6 structure (JCPDS NO. 39-0256) (Fig. 1b). The identified characteristic reflections were positioned at 28.3°, 32.9°, 47.2°, 56.0° and 58.5°, which were respectively indexed to the (131), (002), (202), (133) and (262) planes respectively. Moreover, there was no obvious difference between the XRD patterns (Fig. 1b) of CQDs/HBWO-18 and HBWO, which demonstrated that calcination had a negligible effect on the crystalline phase of HBWO.

The Raman spectra of the samples was presented in Fig. 3 a. The two characteristic peaks of CQDs/HBWO-18 that were not observed in HBWO located at about 1360 and 1630 cm−1, corresponding to the D-band and G-band of carbon, respectively. The intensity ratio of the ID/IG was relatively high at 1.1, which corresponding to CQDs, similar to the previous reports [35]. XPS analysis was used to further investigate the surface chemical composition and valence state of the as-prepared materials to confirm the formation of CQDs. The full XPS spectrum of CQDs/HBWO-18 and HBWO (Fig. 3b) indicates the co-existence of Bi, O, W and C in the prepared catalyst. Fig. 3c shows the high-resolution Bi 4f XPS spectra of the samples. The observed characteristic peaks at 164.1 eV and 158.8 eV were assigned to the Bi 4f 5/2 and Bi 4f 7/2 peaks in CQDs/HBWO-18, respectively, indicating the existence of Bi3+ in the crystal structure. In contrast to HBWO, the Bi 4f peak in CQDs/HBWO-18 exhibited a slight shift near 0.22 eV toward higher binding energies, probably as a result of the interaction between CQDs and Bi2WO6 [36]. The two strong binding energies of W 4f 5/2 and W 4f 7/2 were 37.7 eV and 35.6 eV, respectively, which can be assigned to the presence of W6+ in BWO (Fig. 3d). Similarly, the binding energies of W 4f for CQDs/HBWO-18 also are 0.22 eV higher because of the interaction between CQDs and HBWO-18. The broad peaks of the O1s spectrum (Fig. 3e) for both CQDs/HBWO-18 and HBWO were at 529.5 eV, which corresponded to the peaks of the Bi = O bond in the crystal structure of [Bi2O2]2+. The unique peaks centered at 532.8 eV and 531.2 eV for CQDs/HBWO-18 were identified as the characteristic CO and OH bonds, respectively. The C1s spectra for CQDs/HBWO-18 contained three peaks ascribed to the groups on the surface of CQDs (Fig. 3f). They were located at 284.5 eV, 285.6 eV and 288.4 eV and were assigned to CO, CC and sp2-hybridized carbon, respectively [31]. HBWO presented only one C 1s peak at 284.7 eV, which was assigned to surface extraneous carbon. The above characterization results, along with the results of previous studies, confirm the existence of CQDs in the materials [37], which stemmed from the solvothermal treatment of precursor PVP. From TEM, Raman, FT-IR and XPS analyses, it was clear that CQDs/HBWO-18 consisted of CQDs and Bi2WO6 rather than Bi-based materials and Bi2WO6.

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of CQDs/HBWO-18, HBWO and NBWO are presented in Fig. S3, and the relevant parameters are summarized in Table 1 (Fig. S3 inset table). The similar BET specific surface area (SBET) and isotherm between CQDs/HBWO-18 and HBWO indicated that the crystalline and morphology structure of HBWO barely changed after calcination treatment, which was consistent with the XRD and SEM results. Furthermore, CQDs/HBWO-18 and HBWO exhibited a typical type III isotherm, suggesting that both catalysts had a mesoporous structure with higher BET surface area than NBWO. Thus, NBWO was determined to be a non-porous material with lower SBET and porosity. In contrast, the dramatic improvement in SBET and the pore volume for CQDs/HBWO-18 results in a larger interface for pollutants and promotes adsorption performance, which is beneficial for the photocatalytic reaction process.

In sum, a possible formation process of CQDs/HBWO-18 hollow microspheres was proposed. In the Bi(NO3)3/Na2WO4/EG/EtOH/PVP system, Bi-EG-EtOH complexes were obtained after magnetic stirring (Scheme 1 ). Next, Bi/EO/EtO cores were formed with the solvothermal reaction, followed by the nucleation of these cores. The cores were selectively coated with PVP molecules and further directed the growth of the nuclei, inducing them to form larger Bi/EO/EtO spheres by absorbing Bi ions in the hydrothermal system. PVP is an organic polymer containing both hydrophilic and hydrophobic groups. The hydrophilic groups can interact with the nuclei particles, whereas the hydrophobic groups can ‘control’ the distance among hydrophilic groups to protect the product from agglomeration. In the subsequent process, the Bi/EO/EtO complexes react with (WO4)2− to form Bi2WO6 crystals. As the hydrothermal reaction proceeds, Ostwald ripening occurs, and the Bi2WO6 crystals undergo dissolution–recrystallization. Bi2WO6 crystallites of the inner part gradually dissolve and then become deposited on the outside surface for the growth of larger crystallites at the outer surface of Bi2WO6 crystals. In this process, the absorption of PVP on the surface of Bi2WO6 crystals was driven by the total energy of the system to form CQDs.

The optical absorption of the as-prepared samples was determined by UV–Vis DRS analysis. HBWO and CQDs/HBWO-18 possessed a similar absorption edge at 440 nm (Fig. 4 a). The CQDs/HBWO-18 showed continuous and stronger absorption across the entire spectral region examined because of the presence of CQDs. This indicated that CQDs/HBWO-18 produced more photo-generated electron–hole pairs and had higher photocatalytic performance than HBWO. The NBWO had an absorption edge at 470 nm and stronger visible-light absorption than CQDs/HBWO-18 and HBWO. Based on the plots of (αhυ)2 vs. photon energy (hυ) (Fig. 4a inset), the band gaps Eg of HBWO, CQDs/HBWO-18 and NBWO were approximately 2.88 eV, 2.87 eV and 2.56 eV, respectively. The similar Eg values for HBWO and CQDs/HBWO-18 indicated that the band gap of the HBWO changed little after the removal of CQDs. The valence band X-ray photoelectron spectra (VB-XPS) were used to investigate the valence band (VB) of CQDs/HBWO-18, HBWO and NBWO (Fig. 4b). Electrochemical Mott-Schottky (MS) measurement (Fig. S4) was conducted to investigate the semiconductor type and flat band (E F) potential. Apparently, HBWO was n-type semiconductors, and the potentials of their E F was −0.52 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The E F potentials of HBWO could be converted into −0.30 V vs. RHE [38–41]. The VB positions for both HBWO and CQDs/HBWO-18 were approximately at 1.61 eV vs. NHE. Thus, the CB positions of HBWO and CQDs/HBWO-18 were calculated to be −1.27 eV, according to the formula ECB = Eg – EVB. The results indicated that the band structure of the CQDs/HBWO-18 remained almost unchanged after the removal of CQDs. Moreover, the VB position of NBWO was determined to be 1.44 eV, which meant that the CB position of NBWO was −1.12 eV according to the above formula. The CB of CQDs/HBWO-18 was higher than that of NBWO, suggesting that CQDs/HBWO-18 has greater reducing ability and thus higher reduction efficiency for Cr (VI). Moreover, the VB for CQDs/HBWO-18 was more positive than that for NBWO, meaning that the CQDs/HBWO-18 has a higher tendency to degrade pollutants.

PL intensity is relevant to the recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes. Lower peak intensity in PL spectra indicates lower recombination, which is conducive to higher photocatalytic activity. PL emission spectra of HBWO, CQDs/HBWO-18 and NBWO are shown in Fig. 5 a. The maximum emission peak of as-prepared samples was at 470 nm, and a shoulder peak was at 560 nm at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The intensity of the emission peak for the CQDs/HBWO-18 hollow microsphere significantly decreased compared with that for the NBWO, suggesting that CQDs/HBWO-18 could efficiently accelerate the transportation of the carriers and inhibit the recombination of electrons and holes. However, HBWO had the lowest PL intensity among all prepared catalysts. This might be explained by the fact that virtually no photo-induced carriers were generated on HBWO, which could be confirmed by the photocurrent transient response (Fig. 5b). Both of the working electrodes, the FTO glass coated by as-prepared 5 mg of catalyst (HBWO or CQDs/HBWO-18 film), produced considerable photocurrent with steady and reproducible responses during several time-dependent on/off intermittent Xe lamp irradiation cycles (Fig. 5b). CQDs/HBWO-18 showed a higher photocurrent density than HBWO under the same conditions, indicating that the CQDs enhanced the interfacial charge transfer of CQDs/HBWO-18. The time-resolved PL spectra of CQDs/HBWO-18 and HBWO were collected to further uncover the charge carrier dynamics between CQDs and B2WO6. Both CQDs/HBWO-18 and HBWO displayed the double exponential decay characteristic (Fig. 5c). CQDs/HBWO-18 possessed a longer average lifetime (7.59 ns) compared with HBWO (7.15 ns) (Fig. 5c inset table), suggesting that the introduction of CQDs enhanced the carrier separation and conferred higher photocatalytic performance. In addition, the fluorescence lifetimes τ1 of CQDs/HBWO-18 and HBWO were ~5.65 ns and ~2.45 ns, respectively, because of the direct inter-band exciton recombination. This indicated that HBWO had a longer charge carrier lifetime, which was conducive to the participation of CQDs/HBWO-18 in the photocatalytic process via charge transfer. The fluorescence lifetime τ2 was attributed to the radiative emission via indirect recombination of e− and h+. The estimated τ2 values of CQDs/HBWO-18 and HBWO were ~4.89 ns and ~2.27 ns, respectively, which indicated that CQDs/HBWO-18 had a longer exciton lifetime. In general, the above results showed that CQDs not only enhanced the generation of photoelectron-hole pairs in CQDs/HBWO-18 but also inhibited the recombination of carriers. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopies (EIS) were also used to investigate the electron–hole recombination rate. EIS Nyquist plots of HBWO and CQDs/HBWO-18 are shown in Fig. 5d. The arc radius of CQDs/HBWO-18 was smaller than that of HBWO, which indicated the higher charge carrier transfer efficiency of CQDs/HBWO-18.

The photocatalytic degradation activities of the catalysts were assessed by decomposing TCH. The adsorption–desorption equilibrium for all the photocatalysts was achieved after stirring the suspension in the dark for 60 min (Fig. 6 a). The TCH removal by dark adsorption for NBWO, HBWO and CQDs/HBWO-18 reached ~11%, 13% and 23%, respectively, which demonstrated that the CQDs facilitated the adsorption of TCH because of the π–π interactions [42]. In addition, the existence of CQDs had a marked effect on the photocatalytic degradation activities of CQDs/HBWO-18. Under visible-light irradiation for 60 min, the TCH removal efficiencies were highest in CQDs/HBWO-18 (88.6%), followed by NBWO (76.3%) and HBWO (60.0%). The pseudo-first-order reaction rate constant (k) for the CQDs/HBWO-18 hollow sphere was 0.0277 h−1, which was 2.1 times higher than that of the HBWO hollow sphere (0.0130 h−1). To evaluate the application potential of as-prepared catalysts, Cr (VI) reduction in an aqueous solution was examined under visible irradiation. Approximately ~26%, ~11% and 10% of Cr (VI) were adsorbed by CQDs/HBWO-18, HBWO and NBWO, respectively, in the dark in 60 min (Fig. 6b). Both HBWO and NBWO had negligible photoreduction activity, and 77.5% of Cr (VI) was reduced by CQDs/HBWO-18 in 60 min. The conduction band for CQDs/HBWO-18 and HBWO was positioned at −0.96 eV, which was more negative than that for Cr6+/Cr3+ (+1.05 eV). However, a considerable reduction of Cr (VI) was only observed for HBWO when the tartaric acid was added as hole scavenger to trap h+ and to inhibit the recombination of photoinduced e--h+ pairs. This further demonstrated that CQDs played a major role in separating electrons and holes in the photocatalytic oxidation activities. The effect of initial aqueous pH and initial concentration on Cr (VI) reduction efficiency by CQDs/HBWO-18 was examined in a pH range of 3–9 (Fig. S5a, b).

The simultaneous removal of TCH and Cr (VI) for CQDs/HBWO-18 was evaluated with different initial concentrations for two types of pollutants. In the protocol of single Cr (VI) (Fig. 6c), the photoreduction efficiency of Cr (VI) (approximately 18.0%) was much lower compared with the photoreduction efficiency (77.5%) in Fig. 6b. This could be attributed tothe fact that the dosage of photocatalyst was insufficient, as it was only one-fifth of that in Fig. 6b. In contrast, after the introduction of TCH (20 mg/L), the reduction efficiencies of 5 mg/L and 10 mg/L of Cr (VI) reached 84.5% and 76.7%, respectively, even at low photocatalyst dosage. This might stem from the fact that TCH acted as a photogenerated hole scavenger, which improved the efficiency of electron–hole separation. However, when the concentration of Cr (VI) was 10 mg/L in the bi-pollutant system, the photocatalytic oxidation of TCH was slightly inhibited. The slight inhibition in the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/HBWO-18 for TCH could be ascribed to competition for electrons as shown in Eqs. (1) and (2).
(1) O2 + e− →
O2 −
(2) Cr2O7 2− + 6e− + 14 h+ → 2Cr3+ + 7H2O
The removal efficiency of Cr (VI) and TCH decreased as the concentration of Cr (VI) increased from 5 mg/L to 10 mg/L.

E. coli was used as the pathogenic bacteria to detect the germicidal capacity of the as-prepared catalysts. The dark adsorption and light irradiation alone did not damage the microorganisms, as the survival ratios reached ~90% and ~88% for each treatment, respectively. CQDs/HBWO-18, HBWO and NBWO could achieve 99%, 38% and 59% inactivation efficiency, respectively, in 120 min under visible light irradiation (Fig. 6d, Fig. S6). The dramatic improvement in the elimination of bacteria for CQDs/HBWO-18 may have stemmed from the enhanced separation efficiency of photo-induced electrons and holes, which was conducive to the formation of reactive oxidation species (ROSs) to inactivate bacteria. The complete inactivation of E. coli with CQDs/HBWO-18 photocatalysis was also confirmed by the fact that the irradiated E. coli could not be re-cultured (Fig. S7). The simultaneous removal of TCH and Cr (VI) performance of CQDs/BWO-18 was also compared with other previously reported photocatalysts in Table 1. Under mildthecondition of less catalyst dosage, the as-prepared photocatalyst in this study exhibited comparative degradation and reduction performance.

To elucidate the photocatalytic mechanism of the CQDs/HBWO-18 hollow microsphere, scavenger-quenching experiments were conducted to determine the main reactive species (RSs) generated by CQDs/BWO-18 in the photocatalytic process. BQ and EDTA-2Na clearly inhibited the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of TCH, which suggested that the h+ and
O2 – were the main RSs in the photocatalytic oxidation process (Fig. 7 a). For Cr (VI) reduction (Fig. 7b), IPA had a slight effect on photocatalytic reduction efficiency, whereas the KBrO3 and BQ showed strong inhibition, suggesting that the photo-generated e− and
O2 – were responsible for the Cr (VI) reduction, especially e−. The efficiency of Cr (VI) reduction slightly increased after the addition of EDTA-2Na, which inhibited the recombination of e− and h+. According to the scavenger-quenching experiments, the introduction of BQ also reduced the efficiency of Cr (VI) reduction, indicating that
O2 – was also an active species in the Cr (VI) reduction process. A similar pattern has also been previously reported that
O2 − might mediated Cr (VI) photoreduction, leading to promote the separation efficiency of the e−-h+ pairs and to enhance the removal of Cr (VI) [46,47]. After the addition of IPA, the reduction of Cr (VI) changed little. The introduction of EDTA-2Na slightly enhanced the Cr (VI) reduction efficiency in that the reaction between EDTA-2Na and h+ was beneficial to the transfer of photogenerated electrons to Cr (VI).

The ESR spin-trap technique with DMPO in methanol and H2O was performed to further verify the main reactive species generated by CQDs/HBWO-18 and HBWO in the photoreaction process. The
OH and
O2 – signals of HBWO and CQDs/HBWO-18 could be observed under visible light irradiation (Fig. 7c-d), which was consistent with the results of scavenger-quenching experiments (Fig. 7a). However, negligible signals of DMPO−
OH were observed for HBWO (Fig. 7e), and DMPO−
O2 −signals for HBWO (Fig. 7f) were weaker than those for CQDs/HBWO-18, indicating higher concentrations of
OH and
O2 – in the photoreaction process of CQDs/HBWO-18. The results showed that the presence of CQDs was favorable for the generation of the photoreactive species and that CQDs/HBWO-18 had higher photocatalytic activity.

A possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed based on the experimental results (Scheme 2 ). CQDs/HBWO-18 can be excited under visible light irritation to generate electron–hole pairs. The conjugated π structure of CQDs can enhance charge and hole separation efficiency and can also act as a photoelectron reservoir to inhibit electron–hole pair recombination. The surface of CQDs is rich in chemical groups such as hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups, which can act as adsorption sites for Cr (VI). Therefore, photocatalytic reduction occurred on the surface of CQDs rather than Bi2WO6, which suggests that photogenerated electrons migrate from the CB of Bi2WO6 to the CQDs and leads to the reduction of Cr (VI). Moreover,
O2 – radicals would be produced via the combination of electrons with O2 adsorbed on the surfaces of CQDs to decompose TCH. The h+ generated from the VB band of HBWO can directly participate in the photocatalytic oxidation of TCH. Nevertheless, the h+ could not directly oxidize H2O to form
OH, as the VB position for CQDs/HBWO-18 was 1.61 eV, which was lower than OH–/
OH (+2.3 eV vs. NHE). The
OH could be generated in the CQDs/HBWO-18 catalyst to the further reduction of
O2 − [48], as the following Equations:
(3) O2 + e− →
O2 −
(4)
O2 − + H2O →
OH + OH− + O2

The photocatalytic degradation intermediates of TCH detected by LC-MS were shown in Fig. S8, and the proposed degradation pathway was presented in Fig. 8 . TCH was easily attacked by active species, such as
O2 –, h+ and
OH. The degradation pathways could be considered as three routes. For pathway I, TC1 (m/z = 427) formation was occurred first by dehydration. Due to the relatively weaker N-C bond, TC2 (m/z = 321) appeared after further removing of the N-methyl-substituents, which was successively opened to generation of TC3 (m/z = 252). In the pathway II, intermediates TC4 (m/z = 410), TC5 (m/z = 392), TC6 (m/z = 321) and TC7 (m/z = 252) were formed after the dehydration process, deamination reaction and cleavage of carboatomic ring. In the pathway III, TCH was fragmented into TC8 (m/z = 392) via a series of reactions including deamidation, deamination, hydroxylation and carbonylation. Subsequently, it was converted into TC9 (m/z = 365), TC10 (m/z = 306), TC11 (m/z = 278) and TC12 (m/z = 226) by ring-opening reaction, followed by futher destruction to smaller molecular organic compounds, such as TC13 (m/z = 126), TC14 (m/z = 154), TC15 (m/z = 99), TC16 (m/z = 86) and TC17 (m/z = 58). Finally, they were decomposed into NH4 +, CO2 and H2O.

To investigate the stability of CQDs/HBWO-18, five recycling tests of TCH degradation were conducted under visible light irradiation. No considerable decrease in TCH removal was observed under identical experimental conditions after five successive recycles, as the TCH degradation efficiency only varied from 88.6% to 86.1% (Fig. 9 a). In addition, the XRD and XPS analyses were used to compare the physical structure of the fresh and recycled CQDs/HBWO-18 samples (Fig. 9b and c). There were no obvious differences between the fresh and recycled samples, suggesting that CQDs/HBWO-18 had high photocatalytic stability with a long reaction period for the removal of TCH.

In this study, we successfully fabricated the novel CQDs/HBWO-18 hollow microsphere using a one-step solvothermal method in the presence of surfactant PVP. The composite photocatalyst was formed with CQDs anchored on the surface of Bi2WO6 hollow microsphere. The CQDs/HBWO-18 hollow microsphere has more active sites because of its larger specific surface area than NHBWO. Moreover, compared with HBWO, CQDs/HBWO-18 showed enhanced photocatalytic activity for TCH, Cr (VI) and the inactivation of E. coli under visible light irradiation. The increased photocatalytic activity was attributed to the electron reservoir properties and adsorption capacity of pollutants by the CQDs. ESR measurements and quenching experiments revealed that
O2 −,
OH and h+ were the main ROSs present in the CQDs/HBWO-18 system, and a possible mechanism was proposed for the photocatalysis process. Photo-generated electrons were responsible for the photoreduction of Cr (VI), and co-existing TCH played a key role in the removal of Cr (VI). The EIS, the PL spectra and time-resolved PL spectra confirmed the enhanced charge separation. The photocatalytic degradation pathway of TCH was proposed with the results of LC–MS. This study provides a promising approach for constructing high-efficiency metal-free co-catalyst/Bi2WO6 hollow microsphere heterojunction photocatalyst with excellent photocatalytic activity for the simultaneous removal of different pollutants.
